The accuracy of JOD scores is influenced by the choice of image and observer samples. Sampling images and observers from infinitely large populations affects the quality of the comparison task and experiment.
A sample of inexperienced observers generally leads to noisier annotations compared to a sample of experts, requiring more observers for accurate results.
Psychometric scaling algorithms introduce an estimation error that is inversely proportional to the size of the data, impacting the precision of the experiment.
Estimating the difficulty of the comparison task is linked to the process of image sampling, where similarities between images affect the required number of comparisons.
The quality of the experiment is linked to the process of observer sampling, where the expertise of observers influences the precision of the experiment.
Factors such as image sampling difficulty, observer sampling quality, and psychometric scaling algorithm precision contribute to the experiment error, which impacts the estimation of JOD scores.
The confidence interval (CI) of JOD scores can quantify the experiment error, providing a measure of uncertainty in the IQA process.

The original formulation of confidence interval (CI) does not directly apply to PWC experiments due to the error introduced by the image sampling process, requiring practical alternatives for computation.
The error introduced by the image sampling process is not taken into account [38].
A practical alternative for computing CIs is bootstrapping [8].
We follow the approach proposed in [42] and resort to the percentile method of bootstrapping [6].
We repeatedly generate JOD scores by sampling, with replacement, the observer comparison matrices.
The CI boundaries for each image are then defined as the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the JOD scores.
CI limitations. Confidence intervals represent well the sample mean error for independent variables and samples [42].
This behavior makes the image JOD scores somewhat interdependent, which is not apparent in the CIs [42].
To identify which images have a significant difference in quality, we need to analyze the overlap of their confidence intervals [42].
Do we consider the overlap of 20%, 30%, or 60% to be significant? [42].
What to do in case of multiple overlaps? We address these issues by combining two techniques.
